Class TaskPool

java.lang.Object
com.github.mrgarbagegamer.TaskPool
public class TaskPool extends Object
A non-thread-safe, high-performance object pool for recycling CombinationGeneratorTask instances.

Architectural Role

In the ForkJoinPool architecture used by this solver, thousands of CombinationGeneratorTask objects are created for recursive sub-problems. Allocating a new task for each fork operation creates immense pressure on the garbage collector, becoming a significant performance bottleneck.

This class provides a simple and efficient solution by implementing an object pool. Instead of creating new tasks, generator threads acquire pre-allocated tasks from the pool and return them after use. This strategy drastically reduces allocation rates and GC overhead.

Implementation Details

The pool is implemented as a simple, array-backed circular buffer, which provides O(1) time complexity for both get() and put(CombinationGeneratorTask) operations.

Thread Safety

This class is not thread-safe. It is designed to be used within a ThreadLocal context, where each CombinationGeneratorTask thread owns its exclusive instance of the pool. This design avoids the need for synchronization, maximizing performance in the hot path.

Since:
2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
See Also:
  • ArrayPool
  • WorkBatch
Performance Characteristics:
O(1) for both get() and put(CombinationGeneratorTask) operations.
Threading Model:
Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
Memory Management:
Fixed memory footprint based on the capacity specified at construction.

  • Field Details

    • arrays

      private final CombinationGeneratorTask[] arrays

      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) accesses of elements in the array.
      Threading Model:
      Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
      Memory Management:

    • capacity

      private final int capacity
      The maximum number of tasks the pool can hold.
      Since:
      2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
      See Also:
      • arrays
      • head
      • size
      • tail
      • put(CombinationGeneratorTask)
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe as a final field.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

    • head

      private int head
      The index of the next task to be retrieved from the pool.

      The head index tracks where the next available task is located in the circular buffer. It is incremented each time a task is obtained from the pool, wrapping around to the start of the buffer when it reaches the end, implementing a circular buffer mechanism.

      Performance Considerations

      Using a circular buffer allows for efficient use of the pre-allocated tasks, minimizing memory usage while still allowing for fast put() and get() operations. The head index is updated in constant time, ensuring that get operations remain efficient even as the pool fills up and empties out.

      We could use a short for the indices to save a few bytes of memory, but the performance difference is negligible and using an int avoids potential overflow issues in long-running applications (plus, Java treats arithmetic with short values weirdly). We could also use a single pointer for both head and tail, but that would complicate the logic and force an extra arithmetic operation on each operation, which could impact performance.

      Since:
      2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
      See Also:
      • capacity
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access.
      Threading Model:
      Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

    • tail

      private int tail
      The index where the next returned task will be placed.

      The tail index tracks where the next returned array should be placed in the circular buffer. It is incremented each time an array is returned to the pool, wrapping around to the start of the buffer when it reaches the end, implementing a circular buffer mechanism.

      Performance Considerations

      Using a circular buffer allows for efficient use of the pre-allocated array, minimizing memory usage while still allowing for fast put and get operations. The tail index is updated in constant time, ensuring that put operations remain efficient even as the pool fills up and empties out.

      We could use a short for the indices to save a few bytes of memory, but the performance difference is negligible and using an int avoids potential overflow issues in long-running applications (plus, Java treats arithmetic with short values weirdly). We could also use a single pointer for both head and tail, but that would complicate the logic and force an extra arithmetic operation on each operation, which could impact performance.

      Since:
      2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
      See Also:
      • capacity
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access.
      Threading Model:
      Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

    • size

      private int size
      The current number of tasks available in the pool.

      The size field tracks how many tasks are currently stored in the pool. It is incremented each time a task is returned to the pool via put and decremented each time a task is obtained from the pool via get. This field is crucial for ensuring that we do not exceed the pool's capacity and for determining if the pool is empty.

      Performance Considerations

      The size field is updated in constant time during both put and get operations, ensuring that these operations remain efficient. It is also used to quickly check if the pool is empty (via isEmpty()).

      We could use a short for the size to save a few bytes of memory, but the performance difference is negligible and using an int avoids potential overflow issues in long-running applications (plus, Java treats arithmetic with short values weirdly). We could avoid the size field entirely by using the head and tail indices to calculate the size on-the-fly (or by storing the remaining capacity), but that would complicate the logic and add extra arithmetic operations to the hot path of both put and get operations, which could impact performance. We also don't anticipate the size coming down to 0, so the deoptimization risk is minimal.

      Since:
      2025.07 - Custom Generator Pools
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access and updates.
      Threading Model:
      Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

  • Constructor Details

    • TaskPool

      public TaskPool(int capacity)
      Constructs a TaskPool with the specified capacity and pre-allocates tasks.
      Parameters:
      capacity - The maximum number of tasks the pool can hold. Must be greater than 0.
      Throws:
      IllegalArgumentException - if capacity is not positive.
      Since:
      2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
      See Also:
      • arrays
      • capacity
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(capacity) pre-allocation of tasks.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe by nature of construction.
      Memory Management:
      Allocates a CombinationGeneratorTask[capacity].

  • Method Details

    • get

      public CombinationGeneratorTask get()
      Retrieves a task from the pool.

      If the pool is empty, a new CombinationGeneratorTask is created to prevent stalls rather than returning null. This fallback allocation is a performance anti-pattern and indicates that the pool may be undersized for the current workload.

      Performance Considerations

      This operation is on the hot path of combination generation and must be extremely fast. The implementation uses a circular buffer for O(1) complexity. A null check on the retrieved task is avoided, as the pool should never contain nulls if used correctly. The initial size check provides a fast path for the empty-pool case. Removing it could risk size becoming negative and returning a null task, so it is kept as a safety measure.

      Returns:
      A recycled or newly created CombinationGeneratorTask.
      Since:
      2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
      See Also:
      • isEmpty()
      • put(CombinationGeneratorTask)
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) time complexity.
      Threading Model:
      Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
      Memory Management:
      Only allocates if the pool is empty, otherwise reuses existing tasks.

    • put

      public void put(CombinationGeneratorTask task)
      Returns a task to the pool for recycling.

      If the pool is full, the task is discarded and will be garbage collected. This indicates that the pool may be oversized.

      Performance Considerations

      This operation is also on the hot path. A null check is performed as a safeguard against programming errors, though it could be removed in trusted-caller scenarios for a marginal speed gain. The check against capacity is essential to prevent overwriting tasks in the circular buffer, which could lead to corruption.

      Parameters:
      task - The CombinationGeneratorTask to return to the pool.
      Since:
      2025.07 - TaskPool Introduction
      See Also:
      • size
      • TaskPool(int)
      • get()
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) array access and update.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate.

    • isEmpty

      public boolean isEmpty()
      Checks if the pool is empty.
      Returns:
      true if the pool is empty, false otherwise.
      Since:

      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) field access and comparison.
      Threading Model:
      Not thread-safe; intended for use in a ThreadLocal context.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate.

Performance-optimized brute-force solver for hexagonal grid Lights Out puzzles
